planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Governor Mouton is an early to mid-season bloomer making it one of the first camellias to flower in November–January in zone 9.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Governor Mouton is a classic, long-cultivated Camellia japonica with distinctive semi-double to peony-form flowers showing a highly variable, bicolor pattern — the base petal color is deep rose-red to carmine-red with irregular splashes, streaks, and flecks of white that create an unpredictable, painterly, somewhat informal flower. No two flowers are exactly alike, which gives Governor Mouton a loose, old-fashioned charm quite different from the geometric precision of formal double camellias like Alba Plena or Pink Perfection. This variability is part of its appeal: the informality suits cottage garden and naturalistic landscape styles. The cultivar is named for Alexandre Mouton, an early Louisiana governor, reflecting its long association with the Gulf South camellia tradition. The upright to spreading shrub reaches 6–12 feet tall and produces dense, dark-green, glossy evergreen foliage. Governor Mouton blooms in early to mid-season (November through February in zone 9), one of the earlier and more reliable bloomers in the japonica camellia lineup. It shows good vigor and adaptability to Gulf Coast conditions. Apply acidic camellia-azalea fertilizer in spring and early fall; maintain 3–4 inches of pine bark mulch at all times to protect the shallow root system.

common_name: Elegans Camellia (Chandleri Elegans, Pink Anemone-Form Camellia)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Elegans has a spreading, somewhat horizontal habit — allow 6–8 feet of space on all sides at planting to accommodate its eventual spread.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Elegans is one of the oldest and most historically celebrated Camellia japonica cultivars in American horticulture — introduced by English nurseryman Alfred Chandler in 1831 and still one of the finest camellias for landscape and specimen use nearly two centuries later. The large, anemone-form flowers display a distinctive structure with five large, broad outer guard petals in deep rose-pink to shell-pink surrounding a central boss of densely packed, smaller, petaloid stamens (petaloids) in the same pink color — creating a unique, shaggy, high-centered flower form quite different from the flat formal doubles or simple singles of other camellias. The large flowers — up to 4 inches across — are exceptionally showy and produced in mid-season (December through March in zone 9). The shrub develops a characteristically spreading, somewhat horizontal habit that is distinctive from the more upright growth of most japonica camellias, eventually reaching 5–10 feet tall and 6–10 feet wide. 'Elegans Champagne' is a sport with white petals and yellow petaloids; 'Elegans Supreme' has ruffled pink petals. Provide morning sun and afternoon shade in zone 9, consistent moisture, and acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil.

common_name: Adolphe Audusson Camellia (Deep Red Camellia japonica)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Adolphe Audusson is one of the most vigorous japonica cultivars and can become a large shrub or small tree over many years in zone 9 — allow adequate space at planting.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Adolphe Audusson is a classic, vigorous Camellia japonica cultivar valued for one of the deepest, richest red flower colors available in japonica camellias — a vivid blood-red to deep crimson-red in large, semi-double flowers with a prominent central cluster of golden-yellow stamens that creates a striking color contrast. The bold, saturated red flower color and large flower size make it one of the most eye-catching camellia selections for winter color in the shaded garden. Introduced in France in 1877 and still widely grown, Adolphe Audusson is a vigorous grower that develops into a large shrub or small tree in zone 9 conditions, with upright growth to 7–14 feet and dense, dark-green, glossy evergreen foliage. It blooms in mid-season (December through February in zone 9), with peak bloom typically in January. The bold red flower color provides exceptional cut-flower value — the flowers last well in arrangements. 'Adolphe Audusson Variegated' is a sport with red flowers marked with white streaks and spots; 'Adolphe Audusson Special' has darker red, more formal double flowers. Maintain consistent moisture and acidic soil; apply sulfur-based acidifying fertilizer if soil pH drifts above 6.5.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in late winter (January–February in zone 9) before the trees break dormancy in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile, deep soil in full sun; Elberta requires approximately 800 hours of chilling (temperatures below 45°F) — verify local chill hour accumulation before planting.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile, deep soil in full sun; Elberta is the single most historically significant peach cultivar in American horticulture — developed in 1875 by Samuel Henry Rumph of Marshallville, Georgia, from a cross of Chinese Cling × Early Crawford, and for a century it was the standard commercial and home-garden freestone peach across the South. The large, yellow-fleshed, freestone fruit has a rich, full peach flavor with the balanced sweetness and acidity that defines the classic Southern peach flavor profile. Despite the development of countless newer peach cultivars with improved disease resistance and self-fertility, Elberta remains widely grown for its outstanding flavor quality, large fruit size, and proven production in zone 9 with sufficient chill hours. Elberta requires approximately 800 chill hours (hours below 45°F), which accumulates reliably in zone 9a and most of zone 9 during normal winters, though borderline years may occur. Thin fruit to 6–8 inches apart after petal fall to achieve maximum fruit size. Prune annually to an open-vase form to maintain production. Peach leaf curl, brown rot, and oriental fruit moth are common pests; a preventive fungicide and dormant oil spray program beginning at bud swell is essential for disease management.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in late winter (January–February in zone 9) in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile, deep soil in full sun; Redhaven requires approximately 950 hours of chilling — this is toward the upper end of reliable accumulation in zone 9 and may occasionally result in poor bloom set in mild winters.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile, deep soil in full sun; Redhaven is one of the most widely grown and consistently performing freestone peach cultivars in American horticulture — an early-season variety ripening in late May through mid-June in zone 9, which is 2–3 weeks ahead of the main-season peach crop. The medium-large, round fruit has attractive red-blushed skin over yellow background color, with yellow, firm, freestone flesh of excellent quality and rich, aromatic flavor. The early ripening is a significant advantage — early fruit escapes the worst of the summer disease pressure from brown rot and oriental fruit moth that builds through the season. The vigorous tree produces reliably heavy crops with good thinning practices. Redhaven requires approximately 950 chill hours, which is well-suited to zone 7–8 and marginal in zone 9 — growers in lower zone 9 or inland zone 9a should evaluate local chill hour accumulation data (available from county extension offices) before planting. In borderline chill-hour years, Redhaven may set a reduced or irregular bloom. Annual pruning to an open-vase form and consistent summer disease management are necessary for reliable production.
